Thanks you guys, I'm so excited. These are my favorite thing we do in our company, our entire workshops. We do a lot of different types of workshops, and this is our favorite one by far, so you guys, you're in for a treat. We've changed a lot of it up this time versus anything. We ran the same workshop for that last year, and this time is actually a little bit different. So, we'll do some new content. Hopefully it's gonna make things more simple and easier, and I'm really excited for it. I'm glad you guys made it out here. The reason why this is so much fun for me is because normally in our business, we're up behind our computers, and we're super nerdy up there. You can watch us, and we're doing all this stuff like that. You don't always put people behind the thing. You see numbers, you see statistics, you see stuff like that, and this is so fun for me and for our staff and our whole team, because this is where we actually see the people that we're helping to affect, and lives we're changing, and it makes it real for us. That's why we love it, and we have so much fun. So make sure during the next two and a half days you're taking advantage of my staff. I pay them a lot of money, and they're basically years for the next three and a half days. So if you don't use them, then you just wasted my money and your money, and we got some brilliant guys that are here that work for us. Make sure that you're using them. It's also fun because, and I'm gonna introduce Anne and Jen here, and a little bit of them who haven't come to talk, but it's fun that earlier we were filming a video with these guys, and they were showing kind of their case today. They first learned about this whole process and everything a year ago in December. It's like a year and a half ago, and you're gonna see their evolution, their business, but it's so much fun, and so it's so fun for me to have you guys come, and then people come back again and again, and see the evolution of your lives and your businesses. We've got like four or five people here. This is not your first time here, which is exciting. It means it's been good. So you guys are always, as you know, you're always welcome to come back in the future after you've come through our workshop once. So we do this one about three times a year, so it's a lot of fun, and we're just glad to have you guys here for it. So with that, the only other thing I wanted to kind of talk to you guys about the process over the next two and a half days, we've put people through a process, not just a typical seminar. We used to do just seminars. I'd get up here and I'd talk for three days, and by the end of it, I was tired, everyone's tired, everyone went home, and no one did anything. So I'm like, crap, we gotta figure out a way to make this more a process for people who are actually using it, the benefiting, and taking it home and applying it. So we changed the whole process up about a year and a half ago, and the results since then have been just amazing. So what's gonna happen is today, this is the brain dump day, where I'm gonna just dump a whole bunch of stuff on your heads. More than any of you guys are probably able to comprehend or handle, but you're gonna get everything just once, and you're gonna hopefully feel overwhelmed. If not, it means that I'm slipping a little bit, but hopefully we feel overwhelmed. A lot of people tell me it's kind of like sitting with a fire hose like this, trying to take everything in. Howard, was it like that last time for you? This guy's got 30,000 books, and it was like a fire hose to him, so yeah. So it's gonna be a little bit intense, but don't get overwhelmed by that, that's a good thing. That means that we're covering a lot of stuff. Tomorrow what we do is we break everyone down into little groups, and so we're gonna go upstairs, interact with the officers, and you're gonna spend 90 minutes with me, and it'll probably group like four or five of you, 90 minutes with me, and then you'll spend 90 minutes with our copywriter, Kevin, 90 minutes with our product development team, 90 minutes with our SEO guys, 90 minutes with our PPC guys, and we go in groups like that, and it gives you a chance to have in a small group of the people that run my business, looking at your business and what they can do and give you ideas, and so it's really kind of a powerful hands-on experience, we have a chance to really let these guys dig into your business, and if you don't have a business yet, they help you start building one and getting it off the ground. So that's tomorrow. Again, tomorrow we start at eight, and we go to probably five or six, as soon as the group's done. And then Saturday is another fun day, and this is something we added just recently, and the first time we did it, I was kind of nervous, because I wasn't sure how people were gonna react to it, and I had some ideas, and I wanted to do this process, we called it a mastermind group, but we did it a little different than a typical mastermind group. So the first time we did it, we sat down, everyone was in a room like this, and what we do is we give everyone of you guys a chance to come up here and actually present your business to the rest of the group, which I'm sure some of you guys are freaking out right now saying, I'm not gonna talk in front of all these weird people, but I promise you by Saturday we'll all be best friends. And so we do have a chance to actually come up here on the whiteboard and you map out your business, and then what we do is we turn around where all of you guys become coaches. Anywhere we've ever done sports, who are our athletes? Whoever like coached to the camp, like for me we'll coach at wrestling camps, or football camps, or basketball camps. Okay, what I found, I used to go in the summertime and go and coach wrestling camps, I would learn 10 times more from actually coaching the kids than I just teach in my own moves, but I teach and I'm breaking it down, I'm showing them how to do it, and I'm like, oh, that's why that works, and suddenly I knew something better. So we do on the third days, we turn it around, and we make the process where you guys actually have all the chance to become coaches. So the first time we did this, went through the process, and there's a guy sitting right there in your chair, and a pretty successful guy, and he's sitting in the front, and I'm in the back just goofing off, playing on my phone, not commenting anything, and the guy's up here writing their businesses, and people are kind of giving comments, and I'm just dead silent in the back. Because what I don't want, I don't want people using me as a crutch, that's what always happens. So I don't say word, and first this guy, he was kind of getting all fidgety and agitated, and he kept looking back at me, and he was just like, I could tell he was kind of upset, and the first person got down and went down, the second person got down, and he was getting like, actually he looks in the back, and he's just like, he's just like, how's Larry going to meet me? He's like, do something, and I'm like, I like I waved at him, and you know, just kind of egged him on a little bit, and he was just so angry, and after about four or five presentations like that, you saw him like vividly just like, in his chair, just so pissed off, I find like his hand on the desk, and he's like, look dude, what you gotta do is this, and he started coaching the guy through the process, right? You need to do this, and you do this, and he starts like coaching this guy, and the guy's like, oh it's a great, and he starts changing this whole thing, and the next day he gets up, and he's like again, taking over control, and then he starts coaching it, and after about three or four more sessions, then we broke for lunch, and we broke for lunch, and he pulled me side out side, he said, Russell, you're brilliant. He's like, I was about to, I was so pissed earlier today, I was in a walkout and asked for a refund, because I was so mad that I paid all this money to come here and to learn from you, and you're sitting back there playing games, and talking, and goofing off, and I was just so angry because I wanted you to be coaching all these people, and I wanted you to coach me when I got up there, and he's like, but I finally started coaching this guy, and he said, I learned more in that last, like hour and a half of me coaching people, I did the entire rest of the weekend before, he's like, I learned more about myself, I learned more about the process, and he's like, you're brilliant, I can't believe that you did that, and I was like, oh good, I'm glad that works, I was like our first dry run, so luckily it worked, and since then we keep doing it, and people love it, and a lot of people, surprisingly, they always tell me that the third day is the best day, which is sad, since I'm not talking at all, but anyway, take that for what it's worth, okay. All right, so we're gonna cover a lot of stuff today. This time, like I mentioned, we changed up the content of the workshop a little bit, and the reason why is, I wanted to make a simpler process for the beginning. Now, some of you guys are in information type businesses, some of you guys are not, the beginning is gonna be talking a lot about information type businesses, and whether you're an information business or you're not, just listen to it, take it in, and as the presentation goes on, by the end of the day, you'll understand how it all ties together. It doesn't matter if you're an offline business, an online business, if you're an MLM, if you're an information product, this process is gonna be the same, but I need you to trust me for a minute, so I'm gonna walk through just a hardcore information type business at first, and then from there we'll transition, I know we've got some chiropractors and realtors and things like that, at first you might say, how does this relate to me? But just trust me, and I promise you, go with this ride with me, and at the end of it, you're gonna see how it all flows together. Okay, the first thing I wanna do, and I didn't, normally I have PowerPoint slides, but then I can't really whiteboard, I'm more of a whiteboard guy, so I didn't need to bring slides today, but I put everything in this book here, so hopefully most of the pictures and the visual thing that I normally show, you can see inside the book here, and so what I recommend to do is going through and taking notes while we're going through things in the book. Now, whenever I speak, there's a quote I like to share, and this is from Winston Churchill, he's one of my favorite speakers, the guy is just, you've listened or read the stuff that he's done, it's just amazing, but this is one of my favorite quotes, and it's called, your finest hour, he says, to every man there comes that special moment when he will be figured till he tapped on the shoulder, and offered the chance to do a special thing unique to him, what a tragedy at that moment finds him unprepared or unqualified for that work, which could have been his finest hour. The reason I love that quote is because it's speaking to people like me and you and they're actually here learning the stuff, okay? All of us are out here trying to figure something out. All of us have something inside of us that we can make money with, but second off, we can actually affect somebody else's life, and what a tragedy, if you've got something inside of you, you can change somebody else's life, and you don't have the ability or the know-how to get out there and help that person. When I ever talk about business, the reason why I love business so much is not because of all the money it makes, that part's really fun, I'm not gonna lie, I enjoy the money, love that part of it, but the reason why I like business is because of the other things that happen because of it, because it has the ability to change people's lives, and whatever industry, whatever it is you're selling, you can change people's lives, literally. And if you believe that, it's gonna help you as long term. One of the examples I'd like to share about this is there's a company here in Boise called Idaho Reproductive Center, and they're just a typical company, they do their job every day, and it's not a big deal to them, they do their job. Now, from my wife and I, we spent about two and a half years after we got married trying to have kids, and we kept trying to have kids, trying to have kids, and it didn't work. The process was really fun, but the result never actually happened, and it was really frustrating for me, and so after about two and a half years, we're just like, this isn't working, and so we go to the doctor, and they're like, well, I don't know, try this, and we go home, and they come back in six months, nothing happened, nothing happened, over and over and over again, and just a horrible, horrible process, and maybe when we've gone through that, you guys know the pain of that, when everyone you know is pregnant, everyone's getting pregnant, it's just like, it's not a fun thing at all, really stressful on the marriage and everything, and so anyway, one day my wife was watching Oprah, and she's watching Oprah, and all of a sudden I get a call, oh my gosh, I'm watching this guy on Oprah, and he runs a fertility clinic, and I'm like, oh, that's kind of a cool fertility clinic, she explains, she's like, but guess what? He lives in Boise, Idaho, I'm like, are you serious? Like yeah, so she's like, I'm gonna call right now, so she calls the guy up, and he wasn't available, but the other doctor, which is a woman who was there, she answers the phone, and like, yeah, come on in, so next day we go to Idaho Reproduction Center, we show up, they do a couple tests, they give us some things, I'm not gonna explain those, you guys know what those things are, if you've been through the process, and month number one, nothing happened, month number two, nothing happened, month number three, boom, we got pregnant with twins, and now again, you look at business, okay, business like that, for them, it's just, that's their job, someone comes in, they hand them the paperwork, they hand them the little cup, they, you know, that's the process they go through, it's not a big deal for them, but because that person, because that company was not just shy, and sitting back, saying, hey, this is my business, hopefully people will come to me, but those people were actually willing to get out there and market their products and services, and because they did that, because he was willing to go, I'm getting on Oprah's not an easy thing, if anyone's ever tried it, it's not an easy thing, he had to work hard, a lot of marketing, a lot of money, a lot of effort to get on Oprah, to be able to present his message, but he did that, and because of that, he was the comeback, changed my wife's and I's life forever, okay, and speaking of, we've got the twin boys, we have a daughter now that's three years old, and my wife is actually about to pop any minute now, that's why I actually had myself went up here, just FY, this is the warning, so if I had to run out of here, she was two and a half centimeters dilated yesterday, so if I run out, that's what's happening, and Brent will teach you some stuff about, whatever he teaches people about. So that's just, just FY, I wanna make sure you guys know that, but anyway, so that's my business for me, it's so important, now my role in business is, what I love to do, I'm most passionate about marketing, and I wish I could change the entire world, but I can't, no matter how hard I try, I cannot change the entire world, but what I can do is, because I know how to market stuff, I can come and put on seminars, put on workshops, and teach people how to better market their products and services, and guess what, if someone that ran a fertility clinic came here and learned how to better market their products and services, got the message out and helped one or two or three more people, solve their problems, boom, that's something I was able to help affect, and I know that everyone of you guys have something inside of you, whether it be a talent, a hobby, a profession, there's something inside of all you guys that could change somebody else's life, you guys believe that? You honestly believe that? Okay, if that's true, that's my goal now, is help you guys learn how to market that better so you can get it out to more people, so you can change more people's lives, and if you do that, hopefully your mission will be solved and at the same time, it'll help me as well, so this is really selfish that why I'm doing this, okay, because I wanna make myself feel better by helping more people, that's really why we do it. Now there's another quote, and this is, every time we do workshops, I get one or two people that are just like, well Russell, I don't wanna do that, that's too aggressive, I don't wanna, and if you guys follow my marketing, I'm pretty aggressive with our marketing, and there's a quote from J. Abraham, he says, he says, if you truly believe that what you have is useful and valuable to your clients, you have a moral obligation to try to serve them in every way possible, that's kind of a powerful way to say it, right, well it's just like, hey, if you have something good, you should go sell it, he says you have a moral obligation to your customers to go out there and to tell them about it, okay, and if you start looking at this and start believing that, like I really believe that, I don't think, I mean, again, I like the money behind this, but I really believe that we're changing people's lives, I've seen people like Zan and Jen who've gone in, I've seen what their life looks like now, that's hugely rewarding, fulfilling for me, and so I honestly feel like I have a moral obligation to everyone I meet to help change them, however I can, and it's important for all of us to think about that in our business, to think about it, it's not just, hey, I'm doing this thing for fun on the side, but I've got a moral obligation to someone, who's life am I changing, how am I changing life, how am I making their existence here on Earth better, and it can be something stupid, it can be something, it's a little hair bows, which we'll learn about in a minute, or it can be something as big as fertility, but it doesn't matter, you're changing people's lives, so you're giving people happiness, you're giving them hope, you're giving them something that changes their life, and that's what this whole business is about, so there's my rant at the very beginning, that this isn't just about making money, I'm sure some of you guys are here because you wanna make some money, which is fun, and that's how we keep scoring this game, but that's not the purpose of business or anything, and if it is, I'm gonna talk about your push motives and your pull motives here in a minute, but if your goal, if your whole purpose of being here is to make money, you're not gonna go very far, I've seen it over and over and over again, you do not go far when your purpose is to make money, if your purpose is to help someone or change someone, whatever it might be, that's what is a pull strategy, it's gonna pull you to what you wanna do, and that's what gets people to actually be successful with this business, okay, we see people come through here all the time, year after year, we see people who leave and become really successful, and those who don't, and that's the difference, the people who are leaving, who are not successful are usually the ones who are like, I need to make some money, I need to make some money, people are like, I need to help someone, I'm gonna change someone's life, I wanna add value to the world, those are people that get this and that succeed with it, so I wanna get that mindset kinda in your mind now because it's gonna help you a lot more as we go on. Corvette can't go overseas yet. That's our next goal, but the coolest thing to me is that, like when we first got started, we had 30 some dollars in our checking account, seriously, and the reason that is, Jen was doing some real estate, it's Jen's fault, the reason that is, because I have real deal with it, in that month of December, when he had this phone call with Russell, every single one of my tenants decided not to pay their rent, so it's like okay, we're gonna pay Christmas to the Spencers, we're not gonna pay our rent, so we had seven units at the time, and no rent coming in, plus all the mortgages and all the expenses on the house, and we're down to like 37 bucks, a week and a half before Christmas, and so we really had nothing to lose, and we got started with a little bit more than that because he got paid right after Christmas. You had a mindset real quick, all you just heard the mindset, the difference is we're down to zero, we had nothing to lose, most of us have down to zero, I can't do it, you know just the switch in mindset there, I don't wanna put it in there because that was awesome, anyway, sorry. Yeah, and then once he met Russell, we really thought okay, I mean we're gonna listen to him, we had heard some of his testimonials from other students, and had seen what he had done, and we're like okay, I mean he knows more than we do, we know nothing, we didn't even know what internet marketing was, I mean he had to explain what it was to us, we had no idea, even though we had a website before, we weren't marketing it, we just had a really fancy website. What we're going to start talking about right now then, we are going to start talking about the underachiever process because this is the process they went through with us earlier. Same process I want to take you guys through. If you open your man, you got page number 11. There's a picture there of a guy named Sam Brennan. Anybody heard of Sam Brennan? Couple of you guys? Nobody. OK. So Sam Brennan, he lived in San Francisco during the gold rush days. And he was a very smart entrepreneur. And what happened is all these people out there are looking for gold. And he said, huh, there's a market there. So what he did is he went around. He went to every single business in San Francisco and bought all the shovels. Took all the shovels back to his shop. So he was the only person in San Francisco with shovels. Then he got a little bit of gold. He walked in downtown streets of San Francisco and said, there's gold in the American River. There's gold in the American River. You guys see what people did? Oh, crap, there's gold. Let's go dig it. Come on, come on, come on. Where's the shovel? Where's the shovel's at? And they ran over Sam Brennan's store. They bought all these shovels, went up, started mining for gold. And guess what? Sam Brennan became the first millionaire in San Francisco's history. There's a very powerful lesson to learn from here. Were Zana and Jen tying bows for people? Or were they selling shovels to people? Who wants to be tying bows for life? Who wants to be digging for gold all day long? No, but none of you guys are digging for gold. The moral of the story is that you want to learn about information marketing. That's why it's so powerful. Because what we're doing is we're not digging for gold. We are giving people shovels. The shovel's where the money's at. You can replicate. With digging for gold, you're tying time to hours. You're sitting out there, and I'm getting paid $10 an hour, $50 an hour, $100 an hour, $1,000 an hour. How much money are you making? $1,000 an hour, you're trading time for money. As soon as you break out of that mindset, you start looking at information marketing, now you're not limited by time. You can make as much money as you want. They can be spending 15 minutes a day, but the website's up. It's selling for them around the clock, whether they're there, whether they're in the boysy, whether they're traveling in the Corvette. That's the power of what we're going to be talking about during this weekend. Sam Brannon's a great story, and information marketing is what we're going to be talking a lot about. OK, now what I actually want to talk about is that a lot of people think we're going to talk about the undertaker form, and this whole thing is like that we're talking about being lazy. That's not the goal. The goal of this whole thing is not to be lazy. It's to be able to understand that our goal in life is not to be working for the rest of our lives, right? Which unfortunately, most people don't get that. The whole life, you're like, what do you do for a living? I'm a this. What do you do for a living? I'm a this. Like our whole life, we're conditioned to think that whatever we do for a living is who we are. And that's something we've got to start thinking about. You've got to start thinking about what is it you want to do. Again, with Xan, it was coming back, I want to be an actor. For me, it was I want to start wrestling again. So I built my wrestling gym. Everyone's got some kind of passion, a reason why you're alive, a reason why you're doing this stuff. And if your reason to be in business is because you want to be in business, that's a sad, sad life. So what I want to talk to you guys about right now is figuring out what is your purpose, what is your reason, what's your thing that's going to keep you going. If you look on page number 12, I talk about two things. First off is what dreams are going to push you to success. And secondly, what dreams are going to pull you to success. Pushing someone to success is what we talked about a little earlier, like I've got to get out of debt. Oh, I need to make some money. Oh, I need to cover my bills. If you're in the diet, if you're trying to lose weight, oh, I don't want to look fat anymore. I don't want to do this. I'm going swimming next week. I lose 15 pounds. Those are things that are pushing you, right? If you notice when you have a motivation, it's pushing you what happens. You get psyched up. You get motivated. You're ready. You go out there and you start pushing this thing. You start pushing it. You start pushing it. I want to don't it. I've been on a diet. You lost like five pounds in like 20 minutes. And then you're in donuts like five minutes later. Because your motivation behind it is a pushing motivation. You're pushing yourself up a hill. You're pushing yourself up a hill. I started a new diet again. And my whole plan as a whole diet was I'm going to do a pull strategy. Instead of pushing, I got to lose weight. I need to look better at whatever. I'm going to do a pull strategy. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to find out what things do I like that are really, really healthy that I really, really like? What are my favorite things? Like avocados. I like tomatoes. I like bananas and apples and oranges. Anyway, if you ask anyone in my office this last week, if you're not seeing every single day, every two hours, I walk in the fridge and I have a plate piled full of grapes and bananas and just mangoes and just this amazing fruit. I put it down and I eat that all day long. And then it gets empty. I go fill it back up. And all day long, I'm doing that. And guess what? All I'm doing all day long is eating fruits and vegetables. I like them. I enjoy them. And I get home at night. Guess what? I'm not craving all the stuff I normally crave. OK, and I'm home at night. We threw out all the crap out of our house. We bought a whole bunch of healthy food. And it's right there. And it's pulling me. It's like, oh, sweet. I can have an apple. Oh, I can have a banana. Oh, I can have some mangoes. I can have, and it's a pull strategy. Same thing with your business. I think one of the reasons why these guys are so good is you look at their business. It wasn't like, OK, I've got to go to work now. OK, let's go figure out how to make some money so we can pay our bills. It was like, hey, we're newlyweds. This is a couple. This is a fun thing we're doing together. This is exciting. We're going after a Corvette. This is a fun thing. It becomes that kind of a mindset. When you can structure and position it in your mind where it's a pull strategy, what are the things you want out of it? What are the things you want to focus on? So think about that. And obviously, we're not going to have time to really sit down and write down all your ideas. But when you're home tonight or this weekend, think about that. Think about what are the things that are going to pull me to success? Is it spending time with my wife? What is the thing that's going to pull you? When I first got started in this business, the reason why I got started is the sole reason is because I was wrestling at Boise State University. I'd just gotten married. We'd moved into an apartment. And guess what I found out? You cannot survive even in Boise, Idaho, on 9.15 hours. My wife was making 9.15 hours. And I had two choices. You have to make some money, or you have to stop wrestling. Now, there's very few things in this world that I like more in wrestling. And so I was like, I don't want to quit wrestling. So I got to figure this crap out. And I got out there and I started working and working to pull because I wanted to wrestle. I wanted to wrestle. And that's why it got me up and going. I had a pull strategy, something that was more, it was bigger than just, I need some money to cover the bills. I need some extra money because I want to go surfing next week. Find out what those things are. Because those are the things, if you can get those ideas in Anchorman in your mind and put them out there in front of you, those are the things that are going to pull you through the tough times. Because everyone sells this business as they make a million dollars overnight. The reality is you've got to work really, really hard for two or three months. And then you make a whole bunch of money overnight. How long do you guys have to work before you're making money? Two or three months. And how much time did you put in each day? I was working a full-time job. So I wasn't able to devote as much time as I wanted to. So I would get home at 7. By the time I started working after eating all this stuff, it would be about 8 o'clock. And I would stay up between 2 or 3 in the morning and wake up at 6.30. You work? 7 hours a day. So I'd have to do a night shift. You're 7 hours, you're 15. So a lot of work. Now I want to talk about this for a minute, because I want to talk about the mindset most people have. And I'm hoping you have this mindset. And so I'm going to break it right now. So when I was a boys' estate going to school in my senior year, I was in a creativity class. Nick, where are you in my class? I was in a creativity class. And there was never been a creativity class. So anyway, you're supposed to have a project you're creative on, and you do have a presentation about how creative you are. So I'm like, all right. So at the time I launched this website, it was called the Lost Files. It was my first membership site. So people signed up, and they paid me $27 a month to be part of it. So you launched this site. In the first month I had, I can't remember exact numbers, 200, 300 people that signed up for it, and they were paying $27 a month. And my income was about $15,000 or $16,000 a month off this site. I was so excited. And so I went to my creativity class, and that's what I presented. I said, this is a creative idea I had. I'd launched this business. It's out of work. I mapped out the whole thing on the wall, and I showed it everything. And my teacher, this little tiny Indian dude, he was like, just jumping around so excited. Holy cow, this is so great. He's like, look at the class. You guys, did you see what he just did? He's made a business. He's making $15,000, $16,000 a month. And he built it in two months. This is the greatest thing in the world. Do you guys have any questions for him? And everyone's kind of sitting there half asleep. And this one kid sitting back in his chair, and he raised his hand. And I'm like, yeah. He's like, sounds good, but man, that sounds like a lot of work. And I was like, are you kidding me? I spent two months working really, really hard. And now I have an income stream making me $15,000 a month. I don't do anything for. You are going to spend the rest of your life working 9 to 5, 40 hours a week at a horrible job that you hate, hoping to pull out $50,000, $60,000 a year. That's the reality. So you need to get through the mindset that this sounds like a lot of work, because it's not in the reality, in the grand scheme of things. Yeah, you've got to put in some time. But after you get this up and running in structure, now you can go and spend your 15, 20 minutes a day, because you're just maintaining it, and making sure it keeps working. So make sure you guys have the right mindset from day one. Next thing I want to talk about, this is a big roadblock for people. I want to smash this right now. There's a big difference between having resources and being resourceful. The words sound very similar, right? Resources and resourceful. When I had a chance, and I'll probably talk about this a little later, last summer I got a call in July, I got a call from Tony Robbins' assistant. You guys know who Tony Robbins is? He's launching his reality show on Tuesday on NBC. So make sure you watch. It's going to be the greatest thing in the world. So anyway, I got a call from Tony Robbins' assistant and said, hey, Tony, I'd love for you to come to Fiji and speak at this business master event. Can you make it? I was in a hotel with my wife on our anniversary in Atlanta, and I said, wait, you want me to come to Fiji and to speak at Tony Robbins' event? And she was on the bed, like jumping around. Say yes, say yes, say yes. And I'm like, I don't know how we can do it, but yeah, I'm there. Count me in. So it was a big headache. But anyway, we got there. We got there to the event. And this is one thing Tony talked a lot about. It was a difference between people having resources and being resourceful. One of the biggest excuses I have why people aren't successful every single time is, I don't got any money, Russell. I don't know how to do it on a website. I don't know how to do graphic design. Oh, I don't know how to do this. I don't have the what? The resources, the talents, the abilities. That's the excuse to get over and over and over and over again. Now, what I want to do is I want to smash that quick, because that is the biggest bullcrap excuse that I promise a lot of you guys are telling yourself right now, and you're going to tell yourself throughout this process. I don't have the resources. I don't have the money. I don't have the time. I don't have the whatever it is. Everyone's got an excuse. And I tell you, that's all this is an excuse every single time. If it's important to us, we'll find time. If you guys don't agree with that, it's important you're going to find time. You're going to turn the TV off, and you're going to find some time. Secondly, you don't have any money. There's ways to get money. There's ways to do things without money. What I'm talking about is being resourceful. Now, when I first got started, I was in the same situation. I was going to college. I was wrestling, making $0 an hour. My wife was working, making $9.50 an hour. It didn't cover our payments. We were just like, I didn't have any money at all. So I got online. I was like, I need to figure out how to make some money. I bought this course. I was telling Dave about this earlier today. I bought this course, paid $500 for it. The course was good, but I was just like, it didn't give me what I was hoping for. I was just like, crap, I just paid $500. My wife's going to find out any day now, and I got to figure something out now. This is deadline we got to do it. So I went online, and people who bought this course, they set up a little forum for all the people who bought this course. I went in the course and said, anyone else out there making money with this thing? And everyone's like, no, you? No, no. I'm like, crap, no one's making money with this thing. I'm screwed. So I posted it, and I'm like, look, this is the deal. I'm broke. It's all my money. I got an idea. This is my idea. And I have no way to fund it. I have no idea how I'm going to do it. But this is my idea. I think it's a good idea. What do you guys think? And I got all these people saying, that's a great idea. It's a great idea. Probably hundreds of people, maybe 100, 150 people, said, great idea, great idea. I'm like, OK, cool. So I'm stuck. And most people stop right there, right? Well, great idea. I'm out of money. Let's go home. I got stuck there. I was like, crap. I got to figure it out. So I went back to that forum, and I said, OK, this is the deal. I'm broke. I spent my last penny on this, but I think it's a good idea. You guys think it's a good idea? This is what I want to do. If you will send me $100, you'll be partners with me on this business. And every people who send us $100 will go in there, will collect it, will get a pool of money, and will build this idea. I was like, you guys in for that? And a bunch of people, like, I have over 100 people. Yeah, I'm in. I'm in. I'm in. I'm in. I'm like, huh. So I sent an email. I put up a PayPal button. I'm like, OK, if you're in, here's a PayPal button. Go spend $100 and we'll be business partners. I had 52 people go to that site, spend $100. I had $5,200. Now I had my budget to go and start programming my site. Resourcefulness, not resources. Next story I want to tell you. Tellman Knudsen. Anyone here ever hear of Tellman Knudsen? Has a big multi-million dollar business right now. When I first met him, he came to me and he had an idea. And I thought it was a bad idea. I told him no. But regardless, he didn't listen to me, luckily. I keep telling people that I have bad ideas and they actually work. He went out there and he was the same thing. Completely broke. He had probably $20, $30 a count. He had no money. He said, look, I know I need a programmer. I need a website designer. I need someone to write copy. I got no money, but I got an idea. He had an idea. He went to 20 or 30 designers. He said, I got an idea. I can't pay you, but if you partner with me, we'll be partners on this. And person after person, nope, nope, nope. One guy was like, sure, I'll do it. Boom, got someone to design the entire website for free. They started going out there. OK, I need a programmer. I don't have a program. All I need to know is that any programmers are asking people. I got this idea. I have no money, but do you want to work for free and we'll be partners on this? And like, no, no, no, no. 10, 20, 30, no. One guy was like, sure, I'll do it. Boom, he's got his programmer. Went out there, same thing to the copyright. He got the entire thing. He launched a business his first year to $800,000 and now it is about $6 or $7 million a year off with his business. That's how he launched his business. I don't have the resources, but did he stop? No, he said that I got to find out a way. Anyone here ever heard of Kenneth Cole? Big company? I'm going to slaughter this story, but I'm going to the concepts of the same. So Kenneth Cole is starting this shoe company. Anyone here have Kenneth Cole shoes? I think these might be. I don't even know. So Kenneth Cole is starting this shoe company. He goes out there and there's this big trade show that happens once a year with all the shoe people coming to the scene. He comes in and he's like, I want a booth. It's $18,000 for a booth. He's like, well, I don't have enough money for a booth. And he's like, well, what can I do? Now the reality is most people, in the $18,000 for a booth, they're like, crap, I'm out. And they walk away. Maybe an extra of money. That's when most people stop. Kenneth Cole is a smart guy, so I'm not going to take that guy far another way. So he said, hey, how about I can get a street car and put it out in front of the event and people can come in to see my booth outside the event? So he said, OK. So they went down to the city and asked the city council, hey, can I just rent a street car and put it outside in front of the building and then people can come see it. And they're like, no, you can't. It's against this permit, this permit, this permit. Now what would most people do at that point? All right, sweet, I'm out. Next year maybe I'll have money and leave. Kenneth Cole wasn't, he was persistent. He came back and said, there's nobody in this city that's allowed to have a street car outside. There are. There's only one kind of company that's allowed to have a street car, and that's the movie production company. They can have a street car in the city. He said, all right, I'll be right back. Walked across the street, walked into a stationary store and said, this is my company. It's called Kenneth Cole. I need to change it to Kenneth Cole Productions. I need 15 business cards. They printed out 15 business cards. They said, Kenneth Cole Productions. He walked back across the thing. He said, I'm starting. We just launched a new company. It's called Kenneth Cole Productions. We're in a movie studio. We're filming a big movie thing here, the thing. And they rolled him a permit. He went and got a 40-foot trailer put out right in front of the whole thing. Boom. Sit down in front of this entire shoe convention. He got models to come in and model for free form, shoes and stuff. It was like a movie theater. And people coming in and was able to have the biggest booth in the entire thing. And as you know, Kenneth Cole is a huge, huge, huge billion-dollar company right now. Resources versus resourcefulness. This isn't something we need to ingrain in your guys' brains, because so many times people stop with, I don't got the resources. I don't got the resources. If you do that, you're going to fail. If you say, look, how can I be resourceful? What can I do? That's how you push through those things and how you can make this work. And the next thing, even with that said, a lot of times you're sure you're going to get stuck. Like, well, OK, that's cool, but I don't know what to do. Good news for all you guys here. Who here sucks designing websites? OK, you guys have some hope for you then. That's a good thing. What I found is that most people, they get in this business and they look at it completely wrong. If you're going to start a construction company, what do you do? You hire a contractor. The contractor does a million things. Everyone gets into the internet business. They're like, OK, I'm in the internet business. I can learn how to design a website. I can learn SEO. I got to learn this. And they get in this process, I have to learn everything. What happens? I'm hoping eventually that we get to the right destination at the end of this. And if not, then I apologize for wasting your time. OK. So getting things done. Again, it's important to know that your job in this business is not to be the guy that's doing everything. Your role in this business is to be the person who knows how to outsource and get people to do everything. You're looking at yourself as the contractor. As soon as you look at yourself as a webmaster, now you're saying, I'm a plumber. I've got to go figure out how to install pipes. You don't want to be the plumber in your business. You want to be the contractor, sits at the top and points to people and tell them to do things. Ask anyone in my office. All I do is sit there and point at people. People are like, Russell, you're useless. You don't do anything. I'm like, I know, but I'm good at pointing at people. That's what you want to be, is the contractor in your business pointing at people. So you need to tell them to find a team or hire a team, but you're just outsourcing things to get things done. They become control freaks, and nothing ever gets done because they got to do everything. The less I do, the more I make. That's the right direction you want to go with. OK. I'm glad I showed up that day, actually. So I'm sitting there in class. And he starts talking about opportunity costs. I didn't know the concept of opportunity costs. And I'm sitting there first. I'm just kind of paying attention. And all of a sudden, the light bulb goes off my head. And he said, opportunity costs. The opportunity cost is the cost. How does it even work? I paid really good attention, apparently. The value of what you're giving up. So I can go to work all day. And I can work on this task. My opportunity cost is what I could have done with that time if I had put it somewhere else. I got that. I was like, huh, it's interesting. So I first built this business the first time. And this is before I learned about outsourcing. I went on. I said, I need a cool website. And I saw this guy had a cool website. So I went and I found some graphic design software. And I set it up. I thought I was pretty good. Paid this guy $97. He came back within an hour and a half. And the entire site made boom. And it was the most beautiful, amazing thing in the world. I was like, crap, that's awesome. And I started thinking. I started calculating in my head. I said, OK, I spent three days on that. Probably 12 to 15 hours a day. So let's say it's 15 hours a day times three. That's 45 hours I spent on that. Now let's just say that I value my time at minimum wage, which is what now? $6 an hour? $7 an hour? OK, $7 an hour. Let's say I value my time at minimum wage. So $7 an hour took me 45 hours. How much money did that cost me to do? It was 45 times 7. Who's our mathematicians? What was it? $315. So $315, if I was to pay myself minimum wage, what it cost me to design my banner. Plus the opportunity cost of all the crap I could have been doing during that time. I thought, this guy paid $97 in an hour. He came back 10 times better than I could ever dream of doing it. And it was done. And I had that. It was my big aha moment. And they're making a lot of money on rental properties. But guess what? They go and mow the lawns themselves. I'm like, dude, you can pay a kid $20 to mow the lawn. Why are you mowing the lawn? Well, I got to mow the lawn because I want to make sure it looks good. Pay that guy $30. He's going to make it look just shiny. Think about the value of your time. Most people do not put a value on the time. So I want to go through the exercise real quick. It's a very simple exercise. The next thing I want to tell you about is what do you personally value your time at? Now, if you've got a job right now, it's really easy to figure out a number. Because what is your employer paying you? If they're paying you $10 an hour, then your time is valued at $10 an hour. That's the value you're putting on it right now. Because that's what you're trading for right now. If you're making $100 an hour, making $1,000, everyone's got a dollar amount on your head. So figure it out real quick. What's the dollar amount you had? What do you value your time at? If you valued $1 million, but someone's paying you $100 for it, put $100 down. What's the actual value of your time right now? Let's write that down. Think about it for a second here. Now, after you have that number, that's your magic number. What you want to do is start thinking about things in that way. Awesome. So what I recommend doing is when you're going to hear allot sourcing, go out there and test a lot of people. Because I'm paying $10 for a project, I'd test two people and have both the exact same projects to which one comes back best. And that's what happened. So you're going to find people that suck, but just realize that that guy's really bad. That guy's good. But you're going to find people you like that jive with you, that communicate well with you, and lock those people up because then you want to keep them close to you. That's really how it works. Now, my first product I ever created, this is my very, very first product. It was called Zip Brander. And this was an idea I had. And I was like, this is a multi-million dollar idea. I was so excited about it. I'm not going to try to explain it to you guys because it's not that cool. But I had the idea. So at first I was like, oh, and I kind of walked away. There was another resources. Now, a little while later, when I learned about outsourcing sites, I took the same project, posted it on ScriptLance.com, posted it up there. And this is really my project description, in case you're curious. It was on a piece of blind paper. I drew it like that, zip around it. I want to look like this. And there's going to be a button here. And when I click on this button, it's going to go to page like this. And this goes to page two. And then page two would be this. And I just have five or six pages. I just rip in a pen. I took little pictures of it. And that's what I sent to the site. That's as sophisticated as my project management skills are as a programming project manager. Went out there, and I posted it on ScriptLance. And I had people from all around the world bidding. The first guy came in, he bid like $500. That's 10 times cheaper than the Americans. Next I came in, I'll do it for $150. I'm like, heck yeah, dude, this is awesome. Next I came in, hit it going down, down, and finally got this guy named Cyprian in Romania, bid $20 on the project. And I thought, I got $20. My wife will not even know about it. So I went in there, paid this guy $20. He came back, and within a day and a half, he gave me the software, and it worked perfectly. $20, it cost. Same thing that the American guys quoted me, $5,000. $20, I was so excited. I actually went and found another $100. I gave him that $100 bonus. $120 cost me for this first product. That was my first product I ever had. Went out there, similar process, went there and launched it the very first month. I think it was $1,200 in sales. Second month, we had $3,000. Third month, it was $5,000. That was the whole beginning of how I got started in this business. And there I was like, this is awesome. Create another product, another product, and it just started growing from there. But learning how to use outsourcing sites, I can't tell you how important this is. It's so funny because I have my friends and come up to me and they're like, hey, Ross, I know you know a lot about computers and websites. Can you do this? And I'm just like, I don't know anything about computers or websites. Like my parents are like, hey, Ross, can you come fix this thing? I'm like, no. I don't know how to fix that kind of stuff. If I get stuck, I post on this thing and this kind of remaining comes. He logs on my computer and he fixes it and it's great. That's the kind of mindset you guys need to get into. Because again, if you think that you're the ones building this business, you're going to fail this business. There's too many things going on involved. There's too many things involved. There's too much stuff happening. Don't look at that. Your goal here is the entrepreneur. You guys are the entrepreneurs in your business. Your goal is to say, look, this is the vision. This is what needs to happen. You need to understand how it works. But you don't need to be able to do it. You need to understand it so I can say, hey, guy over here, I need a graphic. And there's health habits with everyone that works out. I hired all those guys, though. All the good ones, I lock them up. If they're good, I don't. I've never had a success with a company, really. So what I want to recommend to you guys doing is going out and start trying to find your team of people. Because again, you're not supposed to be the person that does all this stuff. And if you're a designer, I can't be a designer, I need to get a programmer and an entrepreneur in here, someone to be creative. When I was at Tony's event, he went through this process, and made me kind of laugh. It was like a four or five hour long process. He did it with all these big business people. People making tens of millions of dollars. And he sat down in this process. He said, basically there's three types of people in the business. There's the artist, there is the entrepreneur, and there's the manager. So the problem is that everyone who owns their own business, they think they're the entrepreneur. But the reality is most of us are not the entrepreneur. But we think we are because, hey, I'm starting a business. I'm an entrepreneur. But there's different mindsets and different ways people function. He said, you understand there's, again, an artist, an entrepreneur, and a manager. He went through this whole process trying to get everyone to understand what role they should be playing in their own business. People were crying. If you've ever been at Tony Robbins, you know people were crying. It's like this big emotional deal. But it was interesting because people who thought, I'm an entrepreneur, started saying, wait a minute. I'm not an entrepreneur. I'm an artist. My business, that wasn't my big ahazam as in Fiji, was I always thought, hey, I'm an entrepreneur. I'm an entrepreneur, but I'm not the entrepreneur. I'm an artist. Like what I enjoy is this. I enjoy the creative. That's why I have 8,000 products upstairs, half of them themselves, because I'm so excited about creating stuff. Like I'm the creative guy. And that's what I enjoy. And it took me a long time to actually convince myself that I was the artist. That's what it is. But as soon as I understood that, I said, OK, now I need to surround myself with these other kind of people. The people that's going to be the manager. OK. Step in a little bit. How to find a hot market. There's a great book out there called Tribes. Ever heard of Reddit? How are we getting ready yet? You can knock it out like 30 seconds. OK, it's a great book by Seth Godin. And the whole process, it talks about right now, the whole world is broken into tribes. People are congregating together, and they're meeting. And what you want to find, when you try to find a hot market, you try to find a tribe. You're trying to find a group of people that are passionate about something. Some of the criteria. First thing, you need to make sure it has a large number of people. You don't want to be in a tribe that's got like eight people walking around talking about it. How much money that? So you want to make sure it has a large group of people. Second, you need to make sure these people are irrationally passionate about the topic. You know what I'm talking about? You've got friends who are psychotic about some topic, and they talk about it, and they blog about it, and they've got their own websites, and they're obsessed with whatever the topic is. That's a good thing. You want to make sure these people are obsessed. The more obsessed they are about their topic, the more money that you can make within the market. Next thing is that you have disposable income. That's important. A lot of times people want to buy something, but they can't buy something. So you make sure they have disposable income. If they don't have disposable income, it's not good. Next thing is they have their own communities. They need to be grouping together. We're talking about more in the traffic section, and they need to have their own celebrities. That's not necessarily a big thing. They've got like the gurus or whatever niche they're in. That's a good thing. So they're kind of their own celebrities. Again, tribes is a good book. Next thing, where do you find these hot niches? There's a lot of places to look for them. And sometimes it's your own passion, which is a good thing. If you're passionate about potato guns or about yoga or about whatever, your own passion is a good thing. If it doesn't pass this test, you need to find something else. Again, the Scoutmaster one. The Scoutmasters have a lot of extra money. Are they willing to spend money to help make a better experience for their scouts? Not generally. It was not a good market. So places to find them. A good place to go to amazon.com and look at the book sections. If people were writing books about a topic, not always, but usually it means there's some kind of good subculture there. The next thing, clickbank. If you go clickbank.com and click on the marketplace, it shows you products that are currently being sold, info products, and a whole bunch of different niches. Look on the marketplace, it has categories to go through and look at other products people are already selling. One of the best ways to tell if your market is good is if there's competition, which is backwards from most people think. Most people are like, oh crap, someone else has that idea. I'm out. That's the wrong mentality. If someone else has the idea, like sweet, they just validate the idea for me, I can go and copy it now. I mean, model it. Big difference. Have you ever heard of the company Groupon.com? They started 18 months ago. They went from 0 to $1.35 billion in 18 months. So guess what I did? I said, that's a dang good idea. And we modeled it. Modeled it. Look at things that are already successful. Everyone looks backwards. They look at it and they said, oh man, someone's already doing this. Someone's already got a book about how to get a six-pack. How many books on it and how to get six-packs? How much money is made out there? And guess what? Someone wants a six-pack. They're not going to buy one book. They're going to buy every book because people are passionate, especially people in tribes. They're passionate. They'll buy one thing. They'll buy another thing because they're passionate about the topic. Magazine racks. If there's a magazine on a topic, it's a hot market. Now I almost spent a lot of time and money going after athletes and spending money on like focusing on selling to athletes. This far away from launching this whole product line around it. And guess what? Once the magazine rack looked around, there was not a single magazine focused on athletes. Nitch ones, like runners. There wasn't a single one on athletes. And I may be wrong. Maybe not. But look at that. I said, you know what? If there's not a group that really spent enough money to invest in a magazine for a subculture, I doubt it's a big subculture. I started looking more and more into it. And the more after that, because before I was like, this is the greatest niche in the world, I was going that direction, and I didn't look at all the negatives. I got that one blocker at a magazine. I was just like, there's not a magazine for athletes I can find. I went out and started doing more research. And I started looking at most, especially like student athletes, don't have disposable income. They don't have these type of things. Their parents might, and you can tell their parents is different, but the athletes themselves don't have the kind of money. So that's why we pulled out of that niche, luckily. And maybe it was a wrong move, but we did pull out. And I believe that's the main reason I pulled out is because the magazine. It wasn't a magazine. So make sure there's a magazine on the topic. And the more passionate they have, there's usually a group people that are same passionate about that type of topic. OK, listening to other people's problems, who here loves people to hear people complain? You should, because every time someone complains, there is a market there. My first product was off my own complaint. I said, I was buying resale rights to products. I was selling these people's products. And he pissed me off because I paid like $10. I bought resale rights to products. I sold like 10 or 20 of them. And inside the product, he was talking about that person's business and that person. I was branding this other person. I was selling their product. And I said, gosh, I wish there was a product out there that would brand these ebooks. So when someone opened it, they'd see my ad before they saw their ad. And I thought, huh, is there anything out there? No one had a product out there. So that was my first product. I zip branded, a software product. That's what I did. It branded someone's ebook. So they opened it. They saw my ad before they saw the person's product. That was my first idea. It came off my own frustration. I spent a lot of time going to forums, not so much to participate, more just to look at people complain. The more people complain, the whiner about something that's a good sign. Start listening. I tell you some complain. They're like, that's the product I did. That's the product I did. That's a good thing. So turn complaints into products. Washington TV is a great way. Look in your spam filters. You guys ever watch your spam filters? I spend probably more hours each day in my spam filter than do my inbox, for a couple reasons. First off, because it gives me a product I did. Second off, it gives me ideas on headlines, on topics, on so much stuff. Because people who are spending that much money to spam you, those people know what they're doing. I was down in Florida. And one of my buddies, Rich Sheffrin, owns his company, has $10 to $15 million a year. It was a nice house. And then there's Michael Masterson, who's the owner of Agora Publishing, which is a huge, I don't know how much, Agora, $300, $400 million a year company. We drove down. I said, hey, this is Michael Masterson's house. Beautiful house. I was like, dang, that's a big house. We drove down half a block down. And also, I saw these big hotels. I was like, dang, those are some huge hotels. He's like, those aren't hotels. Like, what are those? He's like, those are people's houses. Who freaking lives in the house? I mean, Michael Masterson, $300 million a year. That's his house. That's a big house. This house is 18 times bigger. I'm like, who lives in those houses? He's like, oh, the spammers. They live in those houses. And I'm like, crap. OK, that means something. If you understand that, the spam, as far as research for marketing, spammers are one of the best people out there. They're getting emails sent to you. They're testing niches. They're testing headlines. They're testing from names. They're testing so many elements you don't even know about. I've met a lot of spammers lately and become friends with them. And it's amazing how much data and how much effort they put into that. And so if they're getting into your spam filter, it's a good market research to watch what they're doing. I'm going to Word Tracker and looking for keywords to get and also looking for paid ads. People are paying for ads. That means there's probably something behind it. Otherwise, they're just big dumb companies, a lot of money. But people are paying for ads. It could be a good niche to go after. OK. That's the first thing. Second thing, after you kind of figure out your market, here's the market filters to make sure you're going the right thing. First off, if you figure out this market is going to be good, first thing is go to Word Tracker. And there are a lot of more advanced keyword tools. Word Tracker is like your simple, easy, it's free. It's a good one. So go to Word Tracker, which is free keywords.wordtracker.com. It's there on the page. And look, see how many people are searching for the topic. The lowest I'll ever go after is about 10,000 searches a month. You do keywords to check how many searches are days. Take that daily and times it by 30 and you got your month. If it's under 10,000, it's probably not worth even trying to go after. The community that heard is too small. With the sweet spot, between 30 and 50,000 up to above is kind of where your sweet spot. 30,000 is like a good sized niche. 50,000, 100,000, it starts getting bigger and bigger. So that's kind of the gauge we're looking for. 30 to 50,000 people. OK. Number two, question number two, the next filter is, are members of this market willing and able to pay for information related to this area? Two important words, willing and able. A lot of people are willing, but there's not able. Other people are able, but there's not willing. You need both of those words there. Yeah, I get it. That's not just for one keyword, right? I just tried the core keyword off it. Like, potato guns were looking at 9,000, 10,000, so it's not a big market. If you took all the data, if you took all the spud and everything else, you'd be probably closer to 30,000. But that's what you're talking about, right? So four keywords. I'm looking more like the first two or three major keywords. So, yeah. OK, so willing and able is a big piece of that. OK, so here's some questions. If you're on page number 20, you've got five other questions here. Question number two, does the typical member of your target market have a credit card? It's important. We had a student who came through last time, and he's actually luckily making a lot of money. He doesn't own a dog, but he saw that dog training niche was really good. So he hired someone to make a dog training product. Try to sell it in America, and there's too much competition. Failed at it. But he's Spanish, or Portuguese. Transcribe the whole product to Portuguese. Start selling it. And the first problem he had is that people didn't have credit cards where they were selling it to. And he got this whole process, finally got it online. But do you remember he's doing like 20, 30 grand a month off this one how to train your dog e-books. And the best thing is he doesn't have a dog. I love it. So anyway, but that was one of the things he had was initially people didn't have credit cards. So he had to change a bunch of stuff and get it to the right country, and things like that to make it work. But make sure they have credit cards. Next thing they have, they have disposable income. Everybody here know Kevin Trudeau? I know he screwed over one of my close friends in the room once. Kevin came out with a book called, he has like health cures they don't want you to know about, and diet cures. And he came out with a book called, Debt Cures They Don't Want You Know About. We worked really close with the people who were the back end call center for that. And they were running on TV, and they were making a ton of money in front of everyone who wanted to buy that book. They call them on the phone to try to sell them high-end coaching programs and stuff like that. And I asked them, how did it go? And they said, their word for it, to me, they said, there's never been a customer so predisposed to not have money in the history of the world as the Kevin Trudeau debt cures lead. None of those people have money. They're all in debt. That's why they bought the $2 book. They can't buy a $10,000 coaching program. So the debt market's good if you're doing other things. But if you're selling info products that do debt, it's probably not a good market. Those people don't have money. That's why they're in debt. Anyway, OK. Next thing, to see, can you find direct competitors? Again, a good thing, not a bad thing. You want as many competitors as possible. There's reasons why the niches I play in are how to make money, diet, fitness. Because those in the market are tons of competitors. Tons of competitors because it's a great market. Next thing, can you find four other products that are being sold to your target market? That's question number four. Now what I mean by this is right here, let's just say I'm selling hair bows, right? I'm looking for four other competitors, not specifically here selling hair bows, but four other competitors selling something in the same target market. So who's your target market? Mom's. Mom's, OK? Can you find four other products being sold out there to moms? Obviously, that's an easy one. That's the question you ask yourself. Can I find at least four other people selling to the same target market? This is coming for your joy venture part and a lot of other stuff we're talking about here in the future. But very, very important. And then how much money you're advertising to spend. You can look up, and that will gloss over that. But you can look and see how much money people are spending to add, and make sure that they're actually sending money. Any questions about market? Traditionally, with the market you want to go after is people that are buying how-to stuff. A couple of questions for you guys. Are you selling hair bows? No, we're selling how-to hair bows. We're selling the shovels. We're not doing the thing. So you want to find markets that are interested in buying how-to stuff, those type of things. I know you guys have your how-to-coupon classes you're doing now. Like, things like how-to is the magic word. Let me step aside a quick. I want to talk about this just to relate it to people who aren't doing information marketing businesses. So let's just say, I know we've got some chiropractors in the room. So here's our chiropractors. We've got some realtors. Chores. Chiro. Do we have any people in MLMs right now, network marketing? Come on. You guys can admit it. All right. So MLM. OK. There's a process we can talk about. We have lots of students coming through who normally don't want to admit they like MLM until I admit that I love. Like, I think it's the most beautiful business model in the world. If you're not in MLM, you're crazy beyond all belief. And then the people start to be like, oh, OK, I'm in four of them. But traditionally, the way that people are doing these kind of businesses online, you're setting up and you're driving leads to try to get people to do back adjustments. You're driving leads trying to get people to come look at do listings with you. You're trying to do, generally, to do people sign for your MLM. The way that doesn't traditionally work very well online is driving people into a site just trying to sell chiropractor or realtors or as you're selling your MLM. It's difficult to do. OK. So how many MLMs I've been? I've been a lot of them. Here's your corporate website that you send your people to. And they're ugly as sin. There's no way that anyone's ever going to buy off those things. So what we do and what we teach people to do is you're using information products to get customers to come into here. Well, we want people to pay us money to become one of our future customers. So let's just say, again, I'm in MLM. So let's say I create a product. Let's say, one of the MLMs I'm in, I'm selling some magic juice that cures cancer. Anyone in here in MLM that has a magic juice that cures cancer? Yes. We've got one that'll emit it to it. One back there who won't emit to that noise. I'm looking at you, Chad. Everyone's got some magic juice that cures cancer, right? So what I'm going to do is instead of saying, hey, come to my thing that has this magic juice that cures cancer, I'm going to create an information product called How to Cure Cancer. I'm being facetious, obviously. But how to cure cancer, right? The people who are looking for how to cure cancer are going to come to this product and they're going to be, what, are they passionate about? If you have cancer, you're going to be a little passionate. Yeah. You've got to figure out, you're going to buy this product because you want to figure out how to cure cancer. In this book, you're going to give a bunch of techniques and ways to do that. Oh, and by the way, one of the techniques in here is the supplement that we sell over here. What happened? Now, someone come by this product, they get the product, they start learning about things, and you call on the phone and say, hey, I see that you're concerned about cancer obviously, and you may have cancer, and we've got this great product, and now you have a lead to sell into your MLM. If you're in MLM, the worst thing in the world is go out and talk to your friends and family members, right? Hey, so I got this magic pill. Do you want to take it and then make some money with it? I get those conversations more than anyone you will ever know on Earth. Because I get it from my friends and people who don't like me all day long. We have Nicole, if you met Nicole, she's like our screener. I would say on an average week, she's screening between 30 and 50 people, telling them no, Russell will not join this or that or whatever it is. Because I get so many things. But again, you're creating information product to get someone to raise their hand. Because the worst thing in the world is go out there and try to get your friends and family. Get people who are already interested in the topic, sell them a product about the topic, sell a $27 hairball product, and there's an MLM for hairball. I know it's a scrapbook in MLM. You guys should be in it. You should join underneath me and my wife's in it. And then you guys can build the whole downline. But think about it. They call them on the phone, hey, you tie hairballs. I mean, this is actually this really cool company. We do scrapbooking stuff. We do scrapbooking parties. Boom, that's the perfect lead that's going to sign up. You talk to your friends about scrapbooking. I'm going to be like, Zan, you first hairball, is there anyone who's scrapbooking? Are you serious? So that's the way we do here. Same thing with real, with chiropractor, with the traditional business. Information products drive people in the traditional business as well. If I wrote the book on the best way to buy or sell a house in Boise in 2010, that was my book. And I'm out there selling that. People are giving it away for that matter. People are looking for they want to buy a house. And also, the guy who wrote the book on how to buy a house, who actually helped me buy a house, that brings a lot of credibility to the table. That's why these info products we're talking about, they don't just work and I'm selling information product business. They're lead gens for any other type of business in the world. When I was in Fiji, I spoke to Tony Robbins' company. There was a seminar. There was a single person in the audience that carried out information marketing. When I talked about whole time information marketing, I said, look, people don't not go to the internet to buy stuff. People go to the internet to get information about things so they can go and they can buy stuff. You want to be the information channel in the middle, because if you control the information, how easy is now to start telling people what to buy? You guys remember Bill Phillips wrote Body for Life? Bill Phillips is the best selling fitness book in the history of the world. So millions and millions and millions of copies of it. And guess what? People are like, man, he makes so much money off those books. He donated every penny he made of those books to charity. He didn't make a single penny off it. But why did he do that? Why did he write this huge book? And then the book had talked about how you needed to eat two grams of protein for every pound of body weight you weigh. So I'm a 200-pound guy. He means I eat 400 grams of protein every single day to follow the Body for Life diet. Why would Bill Phillips tell me that? Because he owns a company called EES. They say, guess what? We sell protein. So we needed a whole crap load of protein. Bill Phillips was the best information market in the world and he wasn't selling information products. He was selling all these other products, but he would use information as a tool to get to people. Very important to understand that. Any business, if you look at the information, you want to be the channel of people coming for information, and then you take that information, they become leads, and then you can do whatever you want. One of the guys I know, he has a whole business teaching carpet cleaners how to market their business. And he teaches people how to make his little infre... And there's not a big ebook or anything. It's little informational ads where they call and they get a little report that's like seven ways that your carpet cleaning guy is killing your kids or something like that. That's his information product. Now if you're about to get your carpets cleaned, flip through the yellow page also, and there's a big ad that says, hey, get this free brochure, seven ways to make sure your carpet cleaner's not killing your kids. You're going to be like, I'm going to call that real quick before I call this other guy, so I'm going to make sure he's not killing my kids. You call it, you get the infopodic, you get the product here, you look through the pamphlet. OK, crap. Some guys do this and this and this and then, and it's like, you know what, a lot of these guys are here trying to kill your kids. So anyway, you can see where I'm going with this, right? Ken, I don't think it's really kill kids, but I hope I didn't go too far in that one. Ken? All right, so step number one, finding the right market. OK, step number two, your fly capture page. This is your ass campaign, fly capture, whatever you want to call it. Again, you set up really simply. This is one, we created a product called How To Overcome Pornography. We're about to launch this actually probably next week or so. Set the fly capture page, drove traffic to it. And what was interesting is the whole direction we were creating the product was completely changed after we got this campaign back. Our direction, in my mind, I was like, hey, it's dudes who have pornography addiction. They want ways to fix themselves, right? That was my thought. The whole product was created towards guys. Guess what happened after we set this campaign? 90% of the people who came through were wives, who their husbands had pornography problems. They wanted for you to fix their husbands. Do you do your product development a little bit different when that's the purple or who are looking for it? Yeah. Everything was different because of that. We created a much better product because we were talking to the people who were looking. That's why these things are so important ahead of time, is to get the information so you make sure you're talking to the people who are looking, and you're talking to them in the way they're talking back to you. So you set this page. Again, if I set a page like this, I'm going to go and I'm going to buy a domain. I'm going to buy what we've got, overcomponeography.com. I'm going to go to Script Lands, I'm going to say, look, this is the deal, guys. I got $5 for this project.
